did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Multiclass Support Vector Machine (MSVM) and Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) as classifiers for the task of skin cancer classification. It extracts textural features (GLCM, HOG) and color features (color histogram) from skin cancer images using a Weighted Least Squares (WLS) framework, and feeds these features into the MSVM and ELM models to classify four types of skin cancer cells (Squamous cell carcinoma, Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Actinic keratosis) from the DermNet NZ database.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a 'multilayer decomposition aided method' using a Weighted Least Squares (WLS) framework for skin cancer classification. It details a specific methodology involving feature extraction (GLCM, HOG, color histogram) and classification using MSVM and ELM models. The authors report original results, including accuracy metrics (e.g., 94.18% with MSVM) on the DermNet NZ and Skin Cancer Detection datasets, and compare their performance against other techniques, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Texture and Color Feature Based WLS Framework Aided Skin Cancer Classification using MSVM and ELM
brief_summary: This study proposes a multilayer decomposition-aided framework for the classification of skin cancer images using textural and color features. The methodology employs a Weighted Least Squares (WLS) edge-preserving decomposition to separate images into base and detail layers, from which normalized symmetrical GLCM, HOG, and color histogram features are extracted. These features are fed into Multiclass Support Vector Machine (MSVM) and Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) classifiers to distinguish between Squamous cell carcinoma, Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, and Actinic keratosis. The approach was evaluated on the DermNet NZ and Skin Cancer Detection datasets, with experiments conducted over 10 simulations using varying training set sizes. The proposed method achieved an average accuracy of 94.18% with MSVM and 90.5% with ELM on the DermNet NZ dataset, outperforming several state-of-the-art techniques. The results demonstrate that the WLS framework combined with hybrid feature descriptors provides superior robustness and classification performance for skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four classes in the DermNet NZ dataset used for multiclass classification. Accuracy reported: 92.9% (MSVM), 89.8% (ELM) with 40 training images.
One of the four classes in the DermNet NZ dataset used for multiclass classification. Accuracy reported: 94.6% (MSVM), 90.8% (ELM) with 40 training images.

One of the four classes in the DermNet NZ dataset and the primary class in the Skin Cancer Detection (SCD) dataset. Highest accuracy obtained in DermNet NZ (95.8% MSVM, 93.1% ELM). In SCD dataset, classified as 'Melanoma' vs 'Non Melanoma'.
One of the four classes in the DermNet NZ dataset used for multiclass classification. Accuracy reported: 93.4% (MSVM), 88.3% (ELM) with 40 training images.
Class used in the Skin Cancer Detection (SCD) dataset for binary classification against Melanoma. Accuracy reported: 95.6% (MSVM), 90.8% (ELM) with 40 training images.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer cell types (Squamous cell carcinoma, Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Actinic keratosis) and Melanoma vs Non-Melanoma status
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of four types of skin cancer cells using the DermNet NZ dataset and binary classification (Melanoma vs Non-Melanoma) using the Skin Cancer Detection (SCD) dataset. Classifiers used are Multiclass Support Vector Machine (MSVM) and Extreme Learning Machine (ELM). Accuracy metrics are reported (e.g., 94.18% for MSVM on DermNet NZ, 97.1% for Melanoma on SCD).

datasets_used.name: DermNet
datasets_used.description: A public collection of skin cancer images captured from different portions of the human body (e.g., nose, eyelid, ear, limb) with a resolution of 150*112 pixels.

use.training: Used for training with varying sizes: 10, 40, and 75 images per class in different simulations/experiments.
use.testing: Used for testing classification performance (accuracy) of MSVM and ELM classifiers.
use.other: Used to assess robustness to illumination, rotational angle, and viewpoint variations.

datasets_used.name: Skin Cancer Detection (SCD)
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing images for binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training with 40 images per class in simulations.
use.testing: Used for testing binary classification performance (accuracy) of MSVM and ELM classifiers.

statistical_methods_used.method: 10-fold simulation with varying training set sizes
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the robustness of the classification method to variations in training set size and to calculate average accuracy metrics.
To quantify the variability of accuracy values obtained across the 10 simulations for MSVM (1.43%) and ELM (1.75%) classifiers on the DermNet NZ dataset.
To report the central tendency of classification performance over 10 simulations and 4 classes for different feature combinations and classifiers.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper reports average accuracy and standard deviation over 10 simulations but does not perform formal statistical significance tests (e.g., t-tests) between methods.

DermNet NZ.experiment: Multiclass Classification (Squamous cell carcinoma, Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Actinic keratosis)
DermNet NZ.notes: The proposed methodology (GLCM+HOG+CH with WLS decomposition) outperformed other techniques. With 75 training images, MSVM achieved 96.26% average accuracy. Component-wise results with 40 training images: GLCM (77.4%), HOG (81.1%), CH (73.2%), GLCM+HOG (88.9%), (GLCM+HOG+CH) without decomposition (91.6%).

Skin Cancer Detection (SCD).notes: Results are described as 'encouraging'. MSVM provided better performance than ELM.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiclass Support Vector Machine (MSVM) and Extreme Learning Machine (ELM)

architecture_details.preprocessing: Weighted Least Squares (WLS) edge-preserving decomposition to separate images into base (smooth) and detail (enhanced) layers

feature_extraction.textural_features: Normalized symmetrical Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) extracted from the detail layer
feature_extraction.color_features: Color histogram extracted from the base layer

elm_details.solution_method: Moore-Penrose generalized inverse of the hidden layer output matrix H

training_details.dataset: DermNet NZ and Skin Cancer Detection (SCD)

classification_or_output_head.msvm_output: Class with maximum distance from the hyperplane
classification_or_output_head.elm_output: Approximation via minimum norm least squares solution

preprocessing_techniques: Cropping the skin portion from the original image
Weighted Least Squares (WLS) based edge-preserving decomposition to separate images into a piecewise smooth (base) layer and an enhanced (detail) layer
Illumination correction via multistage illumination modeling (non-parametric modeling for initial estimation, parametric modeling for final estimation, and removal of illumination variation)
conclusions: The proposed multilayer decomposition-aided framework using Weighted Least Squares (WLS) edge-preserving decomposition to extract textural (GLCM, HOG) and color (color histogram) features from base and detail layers achieves superior classification performance for skin cancer detection.

The method achieved an average accuracy of 94.18% with Multiclass Support Vector Machine (MSVM) and 90.5% with Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) on the DermNet NZ dataset using 40 training images per class.
MSVM provided better overall performance compared to ELM in the multiclass classification of Squamous cell carcinoma, Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, and Actinic keratosis.
The proposed methodology outperformed several state-of-the-art techniques, including those using GLCM, HOG, Color Histogram, and other feature descriptors, on the DermNet NZ dataset.
The method demonstrated robustness to variations in training set size, with accuracy increasing from 94.18% (40 training images) to 96.26% (75 training images) for MSVM on the DermNet NZ dataset.
The approach showed encouraging results on the Skin Cancer Detection (SCD) dataset for binary classification of Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma, achieving 97.1% accuracy with MSVM and 95.7% with ELM.
The highest accuracy values for both classifiers were obtained for Melanoma classes.

The combination of WLS decomposition and hybrid feature descriptors (GLCM, HOG, and Color Histogram) provides superior robustness and classification performance compared to using individual features or methods without decomposition.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a small sample size (359 images for DermNet NZ, 190 for SCD) and low resolution (150x112 pixels), which may limit generalizability to high-resolution clinical dermoscopy images.

The DermNet NZ dataset images were captured from various body parts (nose, eyelid, ear, limb) with varying viewpoints and backgrounds, introducing potential bias related to anatomical location and background clutter.
No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the two specific public datasets (DermNet NZ and Skin Cancer Detection) used for training and simulation.
The paper does not report standard statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) comparing the proposed method against state-of-the-art techniques, relying solely on accuracy comparisons.
The dataset class distribution is imbalanced in the SCD dataset (120 Melanoma vs. 70 Non-Melanoma), which may bias the classifier towards the majority class.
The methodology relies on manual cropping of the skin portion from the original image, which may introduce preprocessing bias or limit applicability to unprocessed clinical images.
The study focuses on four specific skin cancer types (Squamous cell carcinoma, Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Actinic keratosis) and does not address other common skin lesions or benign conditions.

The authors acknowledge that existing techniques often use larger training datasets, suggesting the current approach's robustness might be sensitive to the small training set sizes (10, 40, 75 images) used in simulations.
